quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 7, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Ionic Radius and Isoelectronic Comparisons
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Ionic Radius Trends, Cation vs Anion Size, Isoelectronic Series, Charge Density, Group and Period Patterns for Ions

questions.question: What happens to ionic radius when an atom forms a cation?
questions.explanation: When an atom forms a cation (loses electrons), it typically loses its outermost electron shell and experiences increased effective nuclear charge per remaining electron, causing the ionic radius to decrease significantly.

questions.question: What happens to ionic radius when an atom forms an anion?
questions.explanation: When an atom forms an anion (gains electrons), increased electron-electron repulsion causes the electron cloud to expand, making the ionic radius larger than the neutral atom.

questions.question: Which ion has the largest ionic radius?
questions.explanation: O²⁻ has the largest ionic radius among these options. Anions are larger than cations, and O²⁻ has gained two electrons, creating significant electron-electron repulsion and expansion of the electron cloud.

questions.question: Which ion is the smallest?
questions.explanation: Al³⁺ is the smallest among these options. In this isoelectronic series (all have 10 electrons), Al³⁺ has the highest nuclear charge (13+), pulling the electrons most strongly inward.

questions.question: Which factor most directly affects ionic radius?
questions.explanation: Nuclear charge (along with number of electrons) most directly affects ionic radius. Higher nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, decreasing radius. More electrons increase radius due to repulsion.

questions.question: Across a period, ionic radius generally:
questions.explanation: Across a period, ionic radius generally decreases for cations and then decreases further for anions, though there is a jump between cations and anions. For isoelectronic ions, radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge.

questions.question: Which ion has the greatest radius?
questions.explanation: I⁻ (iodide) has the greatest radius. Ionic radius increases down a group as additional electron shells are added. I⁻ is at the bottom of Group 17.

questions.question: Which ion is largest in Group 2?
questions.explanation: Ba²⁺ (barium ion) is the largest in Group 2. Ionic radius increases down a group as more electron shells are added.

questions.question: Which has the smallest radius?
questions.explanation: Mg²⁺ has the smallest radius among these isoelectronic ions (all have 10 electrons). It has the highest nuclear charge (12+), pulling the electron cloud inward most strongly.

questions.question: Which pair shows increasing ionic radius?
questions.explanation: F⁻ → Cl⁻ shows increasing ionic radius because ionic radius increases down a group. Cl⁻ is below F⁻ in Group 17, so it has an additional electron shell and is larger. The other pairs show decreasing radius: Na⁺ to Mg²⁺ (increasing nuclear charge in isoelectronic series), O²⁻ to F⁻ (increasing nuclear charge in isoelectronic series), and K⁺ to Na⁺ (moving up Group 1).

questions.question: Which element forms the smallest common cation?
questions.explanation: Lithium (Li) forms the smallest common cation (Li⁺). Cation size increases down Group 1 as additional electron shells are added. Li⁺ has only one electron shell (1s²), making it significantly smaller than Na⁺, K⁺, and Cs⁺, which have two, three, and four occupied shells respectively.

questions.question: Which is the largest isoelectronic ion?
questions.explanation: O²⁻ is the largest among these isoelectronic ions (all have 10 electrons). It has the lowest nuclear charge (8+), so the electron cloud is least tightly held and expands most due to electron-electron repulsion.

questions.question: Which ion has the highest charge density?
questions.explanation: Al³⁺ has the highest charge density. It has a +3 charge and a very small radius (highest nuclear charge among these isoelectronic ions), concentrating its charge in a small volume.

questions.question: Ionic radius increases mainly because of:
questions.explanation: Ionic radius increases mainly because of added electrons (forming anions), which increase electron-electron repulsion and expand the electron cloud. Removing electrons (forming cations) decreases radius.

questions.question: Which ion is larger?
questions.explanation: K⁺ is the largest among these options. It has more electron shells than Na⁺, and while both are cations, K⁺ is lower in Group 1, giving it a larger ionic radius than any of the others listed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered ionic radius trends and isoelectronic comparisons! ⚛️📏
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the relationship between nuclear charge and ionic size in isoelectronic series.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on cation vs anion size differences and group trends for ions.
scoreMessages.0: Please review ionic radius definitions, how ions compare to neutral atoms, and isoelectronic series ordering.
